Yesterday the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission proposed a new set of=
 rules requiring public companies to make =E2=80=9Ccertain climate-related =
disclosures=E2=80=9D in their public reports. Here is the 510-page proposal=
. There is a lot. But then, why wouldn=E2=80=99t there be? The SEC has had =
88 years to create and refine a system of rules regulating how companies di=
sclose their financial performance. Now it is building a parallel system re=
gulating how companies disclose their environmental performance. Of course =
that is complicated.The rules=C2=A0will require companies to describe what =
climate-related risks they face and how they manage those risks. If a compa=
ny has a =E2=80=9Ctransition plan=E2=80=9D to adapt to a warming world, or =
if it =E2=80=9Cuses scenario analysis to assess the resilience of its busin=
ess strategy to climate-related risks,=E2=80=9D or if it =E2=80=9Cuses=C2=
=A0an internal carbon price=E2=80=9D to budget for emissions, it will have =
to describe how it uses those tools. Companies that use carbon offsets will=
 have to disclose and quantify that. There will be disclosures in companies=
=E2=80=99 financial statements describing how the financials are affected b=
y climate risks. These rules reference the recommendations of the Task Forc=
e on Climate-Related Financial Disclosures; =E2=80=9CMichael Bloomberg,=C2=
=A0founder and majority owner of the parent company of Bloomberg News, is c=
hairman of=E2=80=9D the TCFD.Companies will also have to disclose their gre=
enhouse gas emissions. Here the SEC references the standards of the Greenho=
use Gas Protocol, which breaks greenhouse gas emissions into three categori=
es: Scope 1 (=E2=80=9Cdirect GHG emissions that occur from sources owned or=
 controlled by the company=E2=80=9D), Scope 2 (=E2=80=9Cemissions primarily=
 resulting from the generation of electricity purchased and consumed by the=
 company=E2=80=9D),=C2=A0and Scope 3 (=E2=80=9Call indirect GHG emissions n=
ot otherwise included in a registrant=E2=80=99s Scope 2 emissions, which oc=
cur in the upstream and downstream activities of a registrant=E2=80=99s val=
ue chain=E2=80=9D). Companies would have to disclose their Scope 1 and 2 em=
issions, and also Scope 3 =E2=80=9Cif material, or if the registrant has se=
t a GHG emissions reduction target or goal that includes its Scope 3 emissi=
ons.=E2=80=9D Large companies will have to get their Scope 1 and 2 numbers=
=C2=A0audited, or rather =E2=80=9Cattested=E2=80=9D by a qualified carbon-e=
missions-attesting firm. (Scope 3 emissions are harder to measure and would=
 not have to be attested, and there is a =E2=80=9Csafe harbor=E2=80=9D for =
companies that try their best but get their Scope 3 numbers wrong.)That is =
a very brief summary; for more you can read=C2=A0Bloomberg News=C2=A0or the=
 Wall Street Journal, or Bloomberg=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9CFive Key Takeaways,=
=E2=80=9D or the SEC=E2=80=99s own summary fact sheet. Here I just want to =
talk about a few high-level themes.First: It seems fair to say, broadly spe=
aking, that a lot of investors want this sort of disclosure. ESG =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0environmental, social and governance investing =E2=80=94=C2=A0is hot =
right now, and many investors have pushed many companies to make many discl=
osures like this.In a sense, this is an argument for regulation. The SEC is=
 basically in the business of making sure investors get the information tha=
t they need, and if lots of investors=C2=A0want=C2=A0some sort of informati=
on, then that is good evidence that they need it. If investors care about a=
 thing, then that thing=C2=A0is material, and companies should disclose it.=
In another sense, it is an argument against regulation. The fact that some =
companies disclose some of this stuff, because investors want them to, sugg=
ests that investors are able to (1) tell companies what they want and (2) g=
et the companies to do it. Companies with material climate risks will get p=
ressure from investors to quantify those risks and explain=C2=A0what they p=
lan to do about them; if the companies say no then they will have a harder =
time raising capital.Or that is the basic theory of =E2=80=9Cprivate orderi=
ng=E2=80=9D: In a world where a smallish number of large investors have a l=
ot of power, why do they need the SEC to tell companies what to disclose? W=
hy can=E2=80=99t the investors do it themselves?=C2=A0The market is working=
; why do we need 510 pages of new regulation?I think there are three main a=
nswers to this:=09Not=C2=A0all=C2=A0investors have a lot of power. Traditio=
nally the SEC has seen itself largely as a protector of small retail invest=
ors, since big investors can better take care of themselves. In theory I su=
ppose that retail investors might care about climate risk at a company that=
 big powerful institutions don=E2=80=99t care about. In practice =E2=80=A6=
=C2=A0this rule proposal does not seem particularly interested in retail in=
vestors? I suppose many retail investors are interested in ESG in at least =
some approximate sense, but caring about environmental disclosures, and qua=
ntifying environmental risk, seems like a purely institutional preoccupatio=
n. These rules are designed to make it easier for professional ESG investor=
s to do their jobs.=09Investors don=E2=80=99t have=C2=A0that=C2=A0much powe=
r. A general theme in U.S. corporate and securities law is that, if shareho=
lders want a company to do something, and the company=E2=80=99s board and m=
anagers don=E2=80=99t want to do it, the shareholders=E2=80=99 ability to=
=C2=A0force=C2=A0them is   rather limited. This is especially true because,=
 even as it is proposing these climate rules, the SEC has proposed other ru=
les to vastly cut back on shareholder activism, making it much harder for s=
hareholders to hold corporate managers to account. (The SEC is planning to =
limit activism through the  swaps disclosure rules and  the 13D rules; we h=
ave   talked about them a   little bit and I hope to discuss them more in a=
 future column.) If you are a big shareholder and you want to get your port=
folio companies to do something =E2=80=94=C2=A0particularly if you want to =
get them=C2=A0all=C2=A0to do it =E2=80=94=C2=A0the most efficient approach =
might be to lobby the SEC.=09It is not easy for big shareholders to act col=
lectively. If big shareholders all want companies to start disclosing more =
stuff about climate, and they team up to pressure those companies, they mig=
ht run into trouble. It might raise antitrust eyebrows for competing instit=
utions to=C2=A0tell competing companies to all do the same thing. Teaming u=
p to pressure a company might run into trouble with the SEC=E2=80=99s limit=
s on =E2=80=9Cgroup=E2=80=9D activities by shareholders. My model of a lot =
of the financial industry is that there are entities =E2=80=94=C2=A0index p=
roviders,   proxy advisers =E2=80=94=C2=A0that are basically in the busines=
s of allowing big investors to act collectively without explicitly teaming =
up with each other. Arguably the SEC is another way for big shareholders to=
 act collectively.Anyway the SEC is fairly clear that it does not think tha=
t private ordering works for climate disclosure, and that the disclosures t=
hat companies now do voluntarily are too limited and inconsistent to be use=
ful for investors. They are also insufficiently=C2=A0legally actionable? If=
 the SEC tells you to write some climate disclosures and put them in your a=
nnual report on Form 10-K, and those climate disclosures are=C2=A0wrong, yo=
u will get very sued. If instead your shareholders ask you to write some cl=
imate disclosures, and you put them in a separate climate report on your we=
bsite, and=C2=A0those=C2=A0disclosures are wrong =E2=80=A6 well, you will a=
lso get quite sued.=C2=A0=E2=80=9CEverything is securities fraud,=E2=80=9D =
as I often say around here, and if a company lies on its website about some=
thing material to shareholders there will be lawsuits. But if it lies in it=
s 10-K about something mandated by the SEC, the lawsuits will be more compe=
lling. =E2=80=9CThe information provided outside of Commission filings is n=
ot subject to the full range of liability and other investor protections th=
at help elicit complete and accurate disclosure by public companies,=E2=80=
=9D says the rule proposal (pages 8-9).A second theme: Why do investors wan=
t this disclosure? One possible answer is, for the same reason they want go=
od consistent financial disclosure: They want to be able to understand how =
companies work, so that they can buy the good ones and avoid the risky ones=
. And most of the proposal is about that sort of thing: Climate risks can a=
ffect a company=E2=80=99s business and financial results, so investors need=

uggestion, in the rule proposal, that that=E2=80=99s not all that=E2=80=99s=

sistency in environmental reporting, saying:We understand investors often e=
mploy diversified strategies, and therefore do not necessarily consider ris=
k and return of a particular security in isolation but also in terms of the=

arable data across registrants.And on page 166, the SEC notes that some=C2=
=A0institutional investors want greenhouse gas disclosure from public compa=
nies because they have committed to their own carbon-neutrality goals:Sever=
al large institutional investors and financial institutions, which collecti=
vely have trillions of dollars in assets under management, have formed init=
iatives and made commitments to achieve a net-zero economy by 2050, with in=
terim targets set for 2030. These initiatives further support the notion th=
at investors currently need and use GHG emissions data to make informed inv=
estment decisions. These investors and financial institutions are working t=
o reduce the GHG emissions of companies in their portfolios or of their cou=
nterparties and need GHG emissions data to evaluate the progress made regar=
ding their net-zero commitments and to assess any associated potential asse=
t devaluation or loan default risks.Notice that this is weird. This is not =
=E2=80=9Cinvestors need this information to understand the company providin=
g the information,=E2=80=9D but rather =E2=80=9Clook, investors these days =
are diversified, and many of them care about the systemic risks to their po=
rtfolios, not about how any one company runs its business.=E2=80=9D If it=
=E2=80=99s material=C2=A0to an institutional investor=C2=A0that its portfol=
io be carbon-neutral, then it needs to know the carbon emissions of each po=
rtfolio company, even if those emissions are not actually material to that =
company.This strikes me as very new! And basically correct, I mean: Investo=
rs are=C2=A0often diversified and systemic these days, so the SEC=E2=80=99s=
 rules might as well reflect how investing actually works. Still it is a no=
vel and surprising concession, asking a company to disclose stuff because i=
t is useful to its shareholders=C2=A0as universal shareholders, not (just) =
because it is relevant to the company=E2=80=99s own business. SEC Commissio=
ner Hester Peirce, in her dissenting statement, objects:Not only does this =
justification depart from the Commission=E2=80=99s traditional company-spec=
ific approach to disclosure, but it suggests that it is appropriate for sha=
reholders of the disclosing company to subsidize other investors=E2=80=99 p=
ortfolio analysis. How could a company=E2=80=99s management possibly be exp=
ected to prepare disclosure to satisfy the informational demands of all the=
 company=E2=80=99s investors, each with her own idiosyncratic portfolio?Thi=
rd: We  talked yesterday about the booming market for environmental, social=
 and governance experts. I wrote that =E2=80=9Cone way to understand the pr=
oliferation of ESG reporting regimes, carbon credit trading systems, etc., =
is that adding a lot of rules and complexity to ESG makes it feel more like=
 finance.=E2=80=9D There is a fuzzy form of ESG in which you try to invest =
in companies that make the world a better place and try to avoid companies =
that make it worse, in each case measured by your own subjective and case-s=
pecific feelings about the company.And then there is this. This is a comple=
x accounting regime for ESG, a legally approved set of Generally Accepted C=
limate Principles, with its own body of technical standards and its own set=
 of climate attestation professionals. There is already a large industry of=
 professionals who certify companies=E2=80=99 environmental behavior; makin=
g those certifications mandatory will be good for business.So page 200 of t=
he proposal discusses the question: Well, how do you calculate your greenho=
use gas emissions?=C2=A0In addition to setting its organizational and opera=
tional boundaries, a registrant would need to select a GHG emissions calcul=
ation approach. While the direct measurement of GHG emissions from a source=
 by monitoring concentration and flow rate is likely to yield the most accu=
rate calculations, due to the expense of the direct monitoring of emissions=
, an acceptable and common method for calculating emissions involves the ap=
plication of published emission factors to the total amount of purchased fu=
el consumed by a particular source. The proposed rules would define =E2=80=
=9Cemission factor=E2=80=9D as a multiplication factor allowing actual GHG =
emissions to be calculated from available activity data or, if no activity =
data is available, economic data, to derive absolute GHG emissions. Emissio=
n factors are ratios that typically relate GHG emissions to a proxy measure=
 of activity at an emissions source. Examples of activity data reflected in=
 emission factors include kilowatt-hours of electricity used, quantity of f=
uel used, output of a process, hours of operation of equipment, distance tr=
avelled, and floor area of a building. If no activity data is available, a =
registrant may use an emission factor based on economic data. For example, =
when calculating Scope 3 emissions from purchased goods or services, a regi=
strant could determine the economic value of the goods or services purchase=
d and multiply it by an industry average emission factor (expressed as aver=
age emissions per monetary value of goods or services).And pages 250 to 259=
 discuss the required qualifications for an =E2=80=9Cattestation provider,=
=E2=80=9D that is, a firm that audits and certifies=C2=A0a company=E2=80=99=
s reported greenhouse gas emissions. The attestation provider has to be =E2=
=80=9Can expert in GHG emissions by virtue of having significant experience=
 in measuring, analyzing, reporting, or attesting to GHG emissions=E2=80=9D=
; it also has to be =E2=80=9Cindependent=E2=80=9D of the company, in the sa=
me way that a company=E2=80=99s financial auditors have to be independent. =
So for instance if you are an environmental consulting firm advising a comp=
any on how to reduce its greenhouse gas emissions, you probably can=E2=80=
=99t=C2=A0also=C2=A0attest to its greenhouse gas report in its SEC filings,=
 because that would put=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cthe attestation provider in the posit=
ion of attesting to such attestation provider=E2=80=99s own work=E2=80=9D (=
page 254).=C2=A0There are obvious problems with an approach to environmenta=
l investing that relies on each investor's fuzzy subjective feelings about =
how pollution-y a company seems to be. There are =E2=80=A6=C2=A0subtler, mo=
re interesting, more=C2=A0financial=C2=A0problems with an approach that rel=
ies on complex accounting methodologies and proxy measurements for emission=
s? It just feels like the industry that these rules will create, the indust=
ry of optimizing this reporting, will be an=C2=A0industry of optimizing=C2=
=A0this reporting, which is not quite the same thing as an industry of redu=
cing greenhouse gas emissions.Fourth: This is entirely disclosure regulatio=
n. The rules don=E2=80=99t require companies to change any=C2=A0behavior,=
=C2=A0except insofar as they now have=C2=A0to measure and report a bunch of=
 stuff. But you can see, from the rule proposal, how a disclosure regulatio=
n can function as a substantive regulation. For instance (page 100):The pro=
posed rules would require a registrant to disclose a number of board govern=
ance items, as applicable. The first item would require a registrant to ide=
ntify any board members or board committees responsible for the oversight o=
f climate-related risks. The responsible board committee might be an existi=
ng committee, such as the audit committee or risk committee, or a separate =
committee established to focus on climate-related risks. The next proposed =
item would require disclosure of whether any member of a registrant=E2=80=
=99s board of directors has expertise in climate-related risks, with disclo=
sure required in sufficient detail to fully describe the nature of the expe=
rtise.Another proposed item would require a description of the processes an=
d frequency by which the board or board committee discusses climate-related=
 risks.=C2=A0The registrant would have to disclose how the board is informe=
d about climate-related risks, and how frequently the board considers such =
risks. These proposed disclosure items could provide investors with insight=

any relevant qualifications of board members.Is the SEC here telling compan=
ies =E2=80=9Cyour board needs to spend 20 minutes of every meeting talking =
about climate risk, and have an environmental expert on the board=E2=80=9D?=
 No, it is not; that is surely beyond the SEC=E2=80=99s authority. But =E2=
=80=A6=C2=A0it is kind of telling them that, isn=E2=80=99t it? If your disc=
losure under this item says =E2=80=9COur board is not informed about climat=
e-related risks in any systematic way, and never really considers them,=E2=
=80=9D that will look bad. Investors will complain, the SEC will look at yo=
u with suspicion, it will be unpleasant. To check this box, you will have t=
o start providing the board with regular reports about climate risk, and de=
voting time to it in board meetings. (This is true even if you are, like, a=
 software company with a modest environmental footprint.) Perhaps this will=
 all be surly and pro forma and your behavior won=E2=80=99t change, but the=
 (reasonable) theory seems to be that if you force boards to talk about cli=
mate change they will end up doing something about it too.Or here is a pass=
age about Scope 3 greenhouse gas disclosure (page 169):Scope 3 emissions ar=
e indirect, but registrants can and do take steps to limit Scope 3 emission=

 operational activities in its value chain that produce Scope 3 emissions, =
it nevertheless may influence those activities, for example, by working wit=

ntities=E2=80=99 Scopes 1 and 2 emissions (and thus help reduce the registr=
ant=E2=80=99s Scope 3 emissions) and any attendant risks. As such, a regist=
rant may be able to mitigate the challenges of collecting the data required=
 for Scope 3 disclosure.Is the SEC here telling large public companies =E2=
=80=9Cyou have to pressure your private suppliers to stop emitting so much =

ying, look, you do what you want, but if=C2=A0you=C2=A0don't=C2=A0pressure =
your suppliers to reduce their emissions, you=E2=80=99re gonna have a heck =

ctively deputizes public companies to be climate enforcers: If their suppli=

at, in the U.S., the SEC is the all-purpose meta-regulator, and here you ca=

A weird thing about being the investment bank advising the seller in a publ=
ic-company mergers-and-acquisitions transaction is that, if all goes well, =
your client will disappear, and at exactly the moment it is supposed to pay=
 your fee. Your fee is due at the closing of the deal, and at the closing o=
f the deal your client stops being an independent public company and become=
s a subsidiary of the buyer. You have spent months negotiating hard against=
 the buyer to extract every last dollar for your client, and then you send =
a bill for all your hard work, and you hope the buyer will pay it. The buye=
r might say no!I mean, the buyer=C2=A0probably=C2=A0won=E2=80=99t say no; y=
ou have a contract, after all, and the contract is still valid after the bu=
yer acquires your client. And it=E2=80=99s not like the buyer will be=C2=A0=
surprised:=C2=A0Your fee will generally be disclosed during the merger nego=
tiations, and in the merger agreement, and in the proxy statement filed for=
 the deal; the buyer will have ample warning that your bill is coming, and =
if it doesn=E2=80=99t object before the closing it=E2=80=99s not like it ha=
s much standing to object afterwards. Still it can make you a bit nervous! =
Carl Icahn once=C2=A0did=C2=A0acquire a public company and then refused to =
pay its bankers=E2=80=99 fee. (They sued and won.)Also  here=E2=80=99s Warr=
en Buffett:Warren Buffett is telegraphing his disdain for Wall Street banke=
rs with an oddball price on his latest multibillion-dollar takeover.The $84=
8.02 for every share that Alleghany Corp. stockholders get from Berkshire H=
athaway Inc. is the result of Buffett balking at the banking fee being set =
aside by the target company -- in this case for Goldman Sachs Group Inc., w=
hich is advising the insurer.=C2=A0Berkshire had offered to pay $850 a shar=
e with Buffett cautioning Alleghany that he didn=E2=80=99t want to foot the=
 bill for the banking fees, according to a person with knowledge of the mat=
ter who asked not to be identified discussing private information. So any f=
ee for a financial adviser would come out of the proceeds for Alleghany=E2=
=80=99s shareholders. The result is spelled out in a regulatory filing: An =
announced purchase price that subtracts roughly $27 million for Goldman -- =
calling attention to Buffett=E2=80=99s stand. =E2=80=A6While deal prices ty=
pically reflect the back-and-forth between buyers and sellers, it gets smoo=
thed over before the deal is struck. Most announcements are priced to avoid=
 clunky numbers after both sides agree on a plan for how advisors are paid.=
 A regulatory filing notes that the price would have been $850 per share bu=
t for the roughly $27 million fee paid to Goldman.Here is that filing: The =
merger agreement says that=C2=A0each Alleghany share will be =E2=80=9Cconve=
rted into the right to receive $848.02 in cash, representing $850.00 per Sh=
are less the financial advisory fee due to the Financial Advisor in connect=
ion with the Merger.=E2=80=9DIn a sense this is a nothing. Goldman is still=
 getting paid.=C2=A0The buyer always effectively pays the fee (generally ou=
t of the target=E2=80=99s cash on hand at closing), the buyer always budget=
s for it, and really it always reduces the amount payable to the shareholde=
rs. It=E2=80=99s rarely explicit; it=E2=80=99s unusual for a buyer to say =
=E2=80=9Cwe=E2=80=99ll pay you $100=E2=80=9D and then find out about the ba=
nkers=E2=80=99 fee and say =E2=80=9Cactually $98.=E2=80=9D In general that =
is sort of an amateurish move: Every seller hires a banker (it is almost le=
gally required!), every buyer knows it will have a sell-side=C2=A0fee to pa=
y, and so every buyer includes an expected fee in its financial analysis be=
fore=C2=A0proposing a price. Renegotiating the price when you find out the =
bankers=E2=80=99 fee suggests that you are acting in bad faith or don=E2=80=
=99t know what you=E2=80=99re doing. You can get away with it if the banker=
s=E2=80=99 fee is egregiously large, or if you=E2=80=99re Warren Buffett an=
d want to be weird just for the sake of being weird.Also the fee is $1.98 o=
n a price of $850?=C2=A0That is not egregiously large; in fact it is a belo=
w-market fee. The stock closed at $676.76 last week, before the deal was an=
nounced.=C2=A0Buffett is paying a 25.3% premium, and Goldman is getting a f=
ee of about 0.23% of the equity value. Without Goldman=E2=80=99s fee, the s=
hareholders would be getting a 25.6% premium =E2=80=94=C2=A0but without Gol=
dman, they might not be getting a premium at all? Like, I dunno, the banker=
s spent the weekend working out a deal, and shareholders came in on Monday =
to find that they were getting an extra $170 per share, but will have to pa=
y Goldman two bucks for it? That seems good? Warren Buffett is kind of tele=
graphing that Wall Street bankers are worth it?Also =E2=80=94=C2=A0presumab=
ly Alleghany has lawyers working on this deal? Presumably they=E2=80=99re g=
etting paid? Effectively, by Berkshire? How does Warren Buffett decide whic=
h fees to deduct and which to pay? Just, like, =E2=80=9Cif we pretend to st=
iff the bankers we will get good headlines, while if we pretend to stiff th=
e lawyers we won=E2=80=99t=E2=80=9D? I don=E2=80=99t know, it=E2=80=99s a s=
trange choice.

You could have a simple model of business school in which, in the olden day=
s, people went to business school to learn how to run a business. Then they=
 used their MBA degree to take over the family pest-control business, or pe=
rhaps to rise through the ranks in some big company. And then in the 1980s =
finance was invented, and business school became training for jobs in the f=
inancial industry. And then in the 1990s tech was invented, and business sc=
hool became training for jobs in the tech and financial industries.=C2=A0Th=
ese developments were in many ways good for business schools (higher salari=
es for graduates, more organized recruiting, professors were more=C2=A0pres=
tigious=C2=A0because they did more math, etc.). But they left a certain gap=
 in industrial America, specifically at its pest-control companies. Who wil=
l take over the family pest-control business, if all the MBAs go into finan=
ce and tech?If you, uh, went to a modern business school, you might say =E2=
=80=9Caha, a gap! We can solve it through financial engineering.=E2=80=9D A=
nd you would be correct! I loved this Wall Street Journal article:Plenty of=
 M.B.A.s finish business school with a hot startup pitch for investors. The=

hing themselves as the investment.Consider the model a SPAC of sorts=E2=80=
=94akin to the stock-market trend in which a special-purpose acquisition, o=

 then finds a private business to merge with. In this case, the investment =

oney to search for a privately held, under-the-radar business and run it as=

mic years as investors=E2=80=94awash in capital=E2=80=94look for promising =
places to put it. =E2=80=A6For M.B.A.s, the search-fund model is a way to b=
ecome a CEO and business owner soon after graduation, without starting a bu=
siness from scratch. Though some non-M.B.A.s have led search funds, recent =
M.B.A. graduates have used the networking and mentoring support they get fr=
om their business schools to start the majority of them. =E2=80=A6The compa=
nies that M.B.A. searchers target aren=E2=80=99t flashy startups or well-kn=
own brands. Many are family firms without a succession plan or companies to=
o small to attract typical investors. The searchers typically hold the busi=
nesses for six to 10 years before exiting, sometimes selling to private equ=
ity. Recently acquired companies operate in insurance sales, security, soft=
ware services, pest abatement and construction, Stanford said.=C2=A0What a =
great trick! If you get your MBA from Stanford and tell your classmates =E2=
=80=9CI am taking over a family-owned pest-control business in Scranton,=E2=
=80=9D and they have fancy offers from private equity firms and tech compan=
ies, they will look down on you. But if you tell them =E2=80=9CI am running=
 a=C2=A0search fund=E2=80=9D then that is, you know, cool, and finance.

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Securities and Environment Commission</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Yesterday the U.S. Securities and Exch=
ange Commission <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27=
113584.264606/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9uZXdzL3ByZXNzLXJlbGVhc2UvMjAyMi00N=
j91dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9Z292ZGVsaXZlcnk/607f07ceef4b8524a319=
a23cB28cd1af9" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_bl=
ank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; ba=
ckground-color: #ccc;">proposed a new set of rules</a> requiring public com=
panies to make =E2=80=9Ccertain climate-related disclosures=E2=80=9D in the=
ir public reports. Here is <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.c=
om/click/27113584.264606/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9ydWxlcy9wcm9wb3NlZC8yMD=
IyLzMzLTExMDQyLnBkZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB14c7f791" itemprop=3D"StoryLi=
nk" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text=
-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color=
: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">the 510-pag=
e proposal</a>. There is a lot. But then, why wouldn=E2=80=99t there be? Th=
e SEC has had 88 years to create and refine a system of rules regulating ho=
w companies disclose their financial performance. Now it is building a para=
llel system regulating how companies disclose their environmental performan=
ce. Of course that is complicated.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The rule=
s=C2=A0will require companies to describe what climate-related risks they f=
ace and how they manage those risks. If a company has a =E2=80=9Ctransition=
 plan=E2=80=9D to adapt to a warming world, or if it =E2=80=9Cuses scenario=
 analysis to assess the resilience of its business strategy to climate-rela=
ted risks,=E2=80=9D or if it =E2=80=9Cuses=C2=A0an internal carbon price=E2=
=80=9D to budget for emissions, it will have to describe how it uses those =
tools. Companies that use carbon offsets will have to disclose and quantify=
 that. There will be disclosures in companies=E2=80=99 financial statements=
 describing how the financials are affected by climate risks. These rules r=
eference the recommendations of the Task Force on Climate-Related Financial=
 Disclosures; =E2=80=9C<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/c=
lick/27113584.264606/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLz=
IwMjItMDMtMjEvc2VjLXRvLXJlcXVpcmUtY29tcGFuaWVzLXRvLWRpc2Nsb3NlLXBvbGx1dGlvb=
i11bmRlci1uZXctcGxhbj9jbXBpZD1CQkQwMzIyMjJfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVt=
YWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMjAzMjImdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1=
vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB3dcdaf47" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" item=
scope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decorat=
ion: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #00000=
0; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Michael Bloomberg</=
a>,=C2=A0founder and majority owner of the parent company of Bloomberg News=
, is chairman of=E2=80=9D the TCFD.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Compani=
es will also have to disclose their greenhouse gas emissions. Here the SEC =
references the standards of the Greenhouse Gas Protocol, which breaks green=
house gas emissions into three categories: Scope 1 (=E2=80=9Cdirect GHG emi=
ssions that occur from sources owned or controlled by the company=E2=80=9D)=
, Scope 2 (=E2=80=9Cemissions primarily resulting from the generation of el=
ectricity purchased and consumed by the company=E2=80=9D),=C2=A0and Scope 3=
 (=E2=80=9Call indirect GHG emissions not otherwise included in a registran=
t=E2=80=99s Scope 2 emissions, which occur in the upstream and downstream a=
ctivities of a registrant=E2=80=99s value chain=E2=80=9D). Companies would =
have to disclose their Scope 1 and 2 emissions, and also Scope 3 =E2=80=9Ci=
f material, or if the registrant has set a GHG emissions reduction target o=
r goal that includes its Scope 3 emissions.=E2=80=9D Large companies will h=
ave to get their Scope 1 and 2 numbers=C2=A0audited, or rather =E2=80=9Catt=
ested=E2=80=9D by a qualified carbon-emissions-attesting firm. (Scope 3 emi=
ssions are harder to measure and would not have to be attested, and there i=
s a =E2=80=9Csafe harbor=E2=80=9D for companies that try their best but get=
 their Scope 3 numbers wrong.)</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That is a ve=
ry brief summary; for more you can read=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link.mail.b=
loombergbusiness.com/click/27113584.264606/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNv=
bS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDMtMjEvc2VjLXRvLXJlcXVpcmUtY29tcGFuaWVzLXRvLWR=
pc2Nsb3NlLXBvbGx1dGlvbi11bmRlci1uZXctcGxhbj9jbXBpZD1CQkQwMzIyMjJfTU9ORVlTVF=
VGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMjAzM=
jImdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cC3dcdaf47" itemp=
rop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color:=
 #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; borde=
r-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc=
;">Bloomberg News</a>=C2=A0or <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusines=
s.com/click/27113584.264606/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9hcnRpY2xlcy9zZWMtdG8=
tZmxvYXQtbWFuZGF0b3J5LWRpc2Nsb3N1cmUtb2YtY2xpbWF0ZS1jaGFuZ2Utcmlza3MtZW1pc3=
Npb25zLTExNjQ3ODc0ODE0P21vZD1ocF9sZWFkX3BvczM/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB5ad1=
4659" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" styl=
e=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width:=
 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-=
color: #ccc;">the Wall Street Journal</a>, or Bloomberg=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9C=
<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27113584.264606/aH=
R0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDMtMjEvZml2ZS1rZ=
XktdGFrZWF3YXlzLWZyb20tc2VjLXMtcHJvcG9zYWwtZm9yLWNsaW1hdGUtZGlzY2xvc3VyZXM_=
Y21waWQ9QkJEMDMyMjIyX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5=
ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjIwMzIyJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07cee=
f4b8524a319a23cB648cbebe" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" ta=
rget=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; =
border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style=
: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Five Key Takeaways</a>,=E2=80=9D or the S=
EC=E2=80=99s own <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/2=
7113584.264606/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9maWxlcy8zMy0xMTA0Mi1mYWN0LXNoZWV0=
LnBkZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBed1d3688" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=
=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">summary fact sheet</a>. =
Here I just want to talk about a few high-level themes.</p><p style=3D"marg=
in: 16px 0;">First: It seems fair to say, broadly speaking, that a lot of i=
nvestors want this sort of disclosure. ESG =E2=80=94=C2=A0environmental, so=
cial and governance investing =E2=80=94=C2=A0is hot right now, and many inv=
estors have pushed many companies to make many disclosures like this.</p><p=
 style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In a sense, this is an argument for regulation. =
The SEC is basically in the business of making sure investors get the infor=
mation that they need, and if lots of investors=C2=A0<em>want=C2=A0</em>som=
e sort of information, then that is good evidence that they need it. If inv=
estors care about a thing, then that thing=C2=A0is material, and companies =
should disclose it.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In another sense, it is=
 an argument against regulation. The fact that some companies disclose some=
 of this stuff, because investors want them to, suggests that investors are=
 able to (1) tell companies what they want and (2) get the companies to do =
it. Companies with material climate risks will get pressure from investors =
to quantify those risks and explain=C2=A0what they plan to do about them; i=
f the companies say no then they will have a harder time raising capital.</=
p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Or that is the basic theory of =E2=80=9Cpriv=
ate ordering=E2=80=9D: In a world where a smallish number of large investor=
s have a lot of power, why do they need the SEC to tell companies what to d=
isclose? Why can=E2=80=99t the investors do it themselves?=C2=A0The market =
is working; why do we need 510 pages of new regulation?</p><p style=3D"marg=
in: 16px 0;">I think there are three main answers to this:</p><ol style=3D"=
list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=09<li style=
=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Not=C2=A0<em>all=C2=A0</em>investors have a lot of=
 power. Traditionally the SEC has seen itself largely as a protector of sma=
ll retail investors, since big investors can better take care of themselves=
. In theory I suppose that retail investors might care about climate risk a=
t a company that big powerful institutions don=E2=80=99t care about. In pra=
ctice =E2=80=A6=C2=A0this rule proposal does not seem particularly interest=
ed in retail investors? I suppose many retail investors are interested in E=
SG in at least some approximate sense, but caring about environmental <em>d=
isclosures</em>, and quantifying environmental risk, seems like a purely in=
stitutional preoccupation. These rules are designed to make it easier for p=
rofessional ESG investors to do their jobs.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bott=
om: 5px;">Investors don=E2=80=99t have=C2=A0<em>that=C2=A0</em>much power. =
A general theme in U.S. corporate and securities law is that, if shareholde=
rs want a company to do something, and the company=E2=80=99s board and mana=
gers don=E2=80=99t want to do it, the shareholders=E2=80=99 ability to=C2=
=A0<em>force=C2=A0</em>them is <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusine=
ss.com/click/27113584.264606/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2=
FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDUtMjcvZXh4b24tbG9zdC1hLWNsaW1hdGUtcHJveHktZmlnaHQ_Y21wa=
WQ9QkJEMDMyMjIyX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3Ns=

, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Sell-side M&A fees</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A weird thing about being the investme=
nt bank advising the seller in a public-company mergers-and-acquisitions tr=
ansaction is that, if all goes well, your client will disappear, and at exa=
ctly the moment it is supposed to pay your fee. Your fee is due at the clos=
ing of the deal, and at the closing of the deal your client stops being an =
independent public company and becomes a subsidiary of the buyer. You have =
spent months negotiating hard against the buyer to extract every last dolla=
r for your client, and then you send a bill for all your hard work, and you=
 hope the buyer will pay it. The buyer might say no!</p><p style=3D"margin:=
 16px 0;">I mean, the buyer=C2=A0<em>probably=C2=A0</em>won=E2=80=99t say n=
o; you have a contract, after all, and the contract is still valid after th=
e buyer acquires your client. And it=E2=80=99s not like the buyer will be=
=C2=A0<em>surprised:=C2=A0</em>Your fee will generally be disclosed during =
the merger negotiations, and in the merger agreement, and in the proxy stat=
ement filed for the deal; the buyer will have ample warning that your bill =
is coming, and if it doesn=E2=80=99t object before the closing it=E2=80=99s=
 not like it has much standing to object afterwards. Still it can make you =
a bit nervous! Carl Icahn once=C2=A0<em>did=C2=A0</em>acquire a public comp=
any and then <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27113=
584.264606/aHR0cHM6Ly9kZWFsYnJlYWtlci5jb20vMjAxMi8wNi9nb2xkbWFuLXN1cnByaXNl=
ZC10by1maW5kLWNhcmwtaWNhaG4tYmVpbmctYS1kaWNr/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBe14b3=
e2a" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;">refused to pay its bankers=E2=80=99 fee</a>. (They sued and <a=
 href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27113584.264606/aHR0=
cHM6Ly93d3cucmV1dGVycy5jb20vYXJ0aWNsZS9jdnItZW5lcmd5LWJhbmtzL29pbC1yZWZpbmV=
yLWJvdW5kLXRvLWdvbGRtYW4tZGV1dHNjaGUtZmVlLWFncmVlbWVudHMtbnktanVkZ2UtaWRJTk=
wxTjBSRDFNNDIwMTQwOTEy/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB51bf708f" itemprop=3D"Story=
Link" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; te=
xt-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-col=
or: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">won</a>.)=
</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Also <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomber=
gbusiness.com/click/27113584.264606/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXd=
zL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDMtMjEvYnVmZmV0dC10aHVtYnMtbm9zZS1hdC1nb2xkbWFuLWJhbm=
tlcnMtd2l0aC1xdWlya3ktZGVhbC1wcmljZT9jbXBpZD1CQkQwMzIyMjJfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1d=
G1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMjAzMjImdXRt=
X2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBf984e8aa" itemprop=3D"=
StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #00000=
0; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-botto=
m-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> her=
e=E2=80=99s Warren Buffett</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"=
>Warren Buffett is telegraphing his disdain for Wall Street bankers with an=
 oddball price on his latest multibillion-dollar takeover.</p><p style=3D"m=
argin: 16px 0;">The $848.02 for every share that Alleghany Corp. stockholde=
rs get from Berkshire Hathaway Inc. is the result of Buffett balking at the=
 banking fee being set aside by the target company -- in this case for Gold=
man Sachs Group Inc., which is advising the insurer.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"m=
argin: 16px 0;">Berkshire had offered to pay $850 a share with Buffett caut=
ioning Alleghany that he didn=E2=80=99t want to foot the bill for the banki=
ng fees, according to a person with knowledge of the matter who asked not t=
o be identified discussing private information. So any fee for a financial =
adviser would come out of the proceeds for Alleghany=E2=80=99s shareholders=
. The result is spelled out in a regulatory filing: An announced purchase p=
rice that subtracts roughly $27 million for Goldman -- calling attention to=
 Buffett=E2=80=99s stand. =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">While d=
eal prices typically reflect the back-and-forth between buyers and sellers,=
 it gets smoothed over before the deal is struck. Most announcements are pr=
iced to avoid clunky numbers after both sides agree on a plan for how advis=
ors are paid. A regulatory filing notes that the price would have been $850=
 per share but for the roughly $27 million fee paid to Goldman.</p></blockq=
uote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here is <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloo=
mbergbusiness.com/click/27113584.264606/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZ=
lcy9lZGdhci9kYXRhLzc3NTM2OC8wMDAxMTkzMTI1MjIwODA3MTQvZDMzNjMzMWRkZWZhMTRhLm=
h0bQ/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB54774f9d" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D=
"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: non=
e !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; borde=
r-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">that filing</a>: The merger=
 agreement says that=C2=A0each Alleghany share will be =E2=80=9Cconverted i=
nto the right to receive $848.02 in cash, representing $850.00 per Share le=
ss the financial advisory fee due to the Financial Advisor in connection wi=
th the Merger.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In a sense this is =
a nothing. Goldman is still getting paid.=C2=A0The buyer always effectively=
 pays the fee (generally out of the target=E2=80=99s cash on hand at closin=
g), the buyer always budgets for it, and really it always reduces the amoun=
t payable to the shareholders. It=E2=80=99s rarely explicit; it=E2=80=99s u=
nusual for a buyer to say =E2=80=9Cwe=E2=80=99ll pay you $100=E2=80=9D and =
then find out about the bankers=E2=80=99 fee and say =E2=80=9Cactually $98.=
=E2=80=9D In general that is sort of an amateurish move: Every seller hires=
 a banker (it is almost <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/=
click/27113584.264606/aHR0cHM6Ly9wYXBlcnMuc3Nybi5jb20vc29sMy9wYXBlcnMuY2ZtP=
2Fic3RyYWN0X2lkPTMwNjE0MQ/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB6f6121d6" itemprop=3D"St=
oryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000;=
 text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-=
color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">legall=
y required</a>!), every buyer knows it will have a sell-side=C2=A0fee to pa=
y, and so every buyer includes an expected fee in its financial analysis <e=
m>before=C2=A0</em>proposing a price. Renegotiating the price when you find=
 out the bankers=E2=80=99 fee suggests that you are acting in bad faith or =
don=E2=80=99t know what you=E2=80=99re doing. You can get away with it if t=
he bankers=E2=80=99 fee is egregiously large, or if you=E2=80=99re Warren B=
uffett and want to be weird just for the sake of being weird.</p><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Also the fee is $1.98 on a price of $850?=C2=A0That is=
 not egregiously large; in fact it is a below-market fee. The stock closed =
at $676.76 last week, before the deal was announced.=C2=A0Buffett is paying=
 a 25.3% premium, and Goldman is getting a fee of about 0.23% of the equity=
 value. Without Goldman=E2=80=99s fee, the shareholders would be getting a =
25.6% premium =E2=80=94=C2=A0but without Goldman, they might not be getting=
 a premium at all? Like, I dunno, the bankers spent the weekend working out=
 a deal, and shareholders came in on Monday to find that they were getting =
an extra $170 per share, but will have to pay Goldman two bucks for it? Tha=
t seems good? Warren Buffett is kind of telegraphing that Wall Street banke=
rs are worth it?</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Also =E2=80=94=C2=A0presum=
ably Alleghany has lawyers working on this deal? Presumably they=E2=80=99re=
 getting paid? Effectively, by Berkshire? How does Warren Buffett decide wh=
ich fees to deduct and which to pay? Just, like, =E2=80=9Cif we pretend to =
stiff the bankers we will get good headlines, while if we pretend to stiff =
the lawyers we won=E2=80=99t=E2=80=9D? I don=E2=80=99t know, it=E2=80=99s a=
 strange choice.</p><table class=3D"liveintent-flex-ad" border=3D"0" cellpa=
dding=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" align=3D"center">
<tr>

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Search fund</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">You could have a simple model of busin=
ess school in which, in the olden days, people went to business school to l=
earn how to run a business. Then they used their MBA degree to take over th=
e family pest-control business, or perhaps to rise through the ranks in som=
e big company. And then in the 1980s finance was invented, and business sch=
ool became training for jobs in the financial industry. And then in the 199=
0s tech was invented, and business school became training for jobs in the t=
ech and financial industries.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">These d=
evelopments were in many ways good for business schools (higher salaries fo=
r graduates, more organized recruiting, professors were more=C2=A0prestigio=
us=C2=A0because they did more math, etc.). But they left a certain gap in i=
ndustrial America, specifically at its pest-control companies. Who will tak=
e over the family pest-control business, if all the MBAs go into finance an=
d tech?</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you, uh, went to a modern busine=
ss school, you might say =E2=80=9Caha, a gap! We can solve it through finan=
cial engineering.=E2=80=9D And you would be correct! I loved <a href=3D"htt=
ps://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27113584.264606/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cu=
d3NqLmNvbS9hcnRpY2xlcy9tLWItYS1zLWxhdGVzdC1waXRjaC1pbnZlc3RvcnMtc3RhcnR1cC1=
idXNpbmVzcy1zY2hvb2wtc3BhYy0xMTY0NzQ1NzI1NQ/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB8a4d25=
e9" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;">this Wall Street Journal article</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Plenty of M.B.A.s finish business school with a hot st=
artup pitch for investors. The latest breed of student-entrepreneur is skip=
ping the startup part and pitching themselves as the investment.</p><p styl=
e=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Consider the model a SPAC of sorts=E2=80=94akin to th=
e stock-market trend in which a special-purpose acquisition, or =E2=80=9Cbl=
ank-check,=E2=80=9D company raises money and lists its shares, then finds a=
 private business to merge with. In this case, the investment vehicle is a =
fund for a newly minted M.B.A. graduate. The M.B.A. uses the money to searc=
h for a privately held, under-the-radar business and run it as chief execut=
ive and part owner.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">These so-called s=
earch funds came on the business-school scene decades ago, but they have ta=
ken off in the pandemic years as investors=E2=80=94awash in capital=E2=80=
=94look for promising places to put it. =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 16=
px 0;">For M.B.A.s, the search-fund model is a way to become a CEO and busi=
ness owner soon after graduation, without starting a business from scratch.=
 Though some non-M.B.A.s have led search funds, recent M.B.A. graduates hav=
e used the networking and mentoring support they get from their business sc=
hools to start the majority of them. =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px =
0;">The companies that M.B.A. searchers target aren=E2=80=99t flashy startu=
ps or well-known brands. Many are family firms without a succession plan or=
 companies too small to attract typical investors. The searchers typically =
hold the businesses for six to 10 years before exiting, sometimes selling t=
o private equity. Recently acquired companies operate in insurance sales, s=
ecurity, software services, pest abatement and construction, Stanford said.=
=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">What a great trick! If =
you get your MBA from Stanford and tell your classmates =E2=80=9CI am takin=
g over a family-owned pest-control business in Scranton,=E2=80=9D and they =
have fancy offers from private equity firms and tech companies, they will l=
ook down on you. But if you tell them =E2=80=9CI am running a=C2=A0<em>sear=
ch fund</em>=E2=80=9D then that is, you know, cool, and finance.</p><table =
class=3D"header" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" cellpadding=
=3D"0">
<tr>

om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Nickel=E2=80=99s=C2=A0back</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Hey it=E2=80=99s <a href=3D"https://li=
nk.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27113584.264606/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21=
iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDMtMjIvbmlja2VsLWlzLXRyYWRpbmctYXQtbG=
FzdC1vbi1sbWUtYXMtYnV5ZXJzLXJldHVybi1hZnRlci1zcXVlZXplP2NtcGlkPUJCRDAzMjIyM=
l9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90=
ZXJtPTIyMDMyMiZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBae1=
33dbb" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" sty=
le=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width=
: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background=
-color: #ccc;"> all fine now</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0=
;">Nickel volumes surged as prices traded within the London Metal Exchange=
=E2=80=99s daily limits for the first time since reopening last week, in a =
sign that the market is starting to stabilize after an unprecedented squeez=
e sent prices spiking.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The flurry of deals =
on Tuesday came as LME prices moved back into line with nickel contracts tr=
ading on the Shanghai Futures Exchange, which remained open during a weeklo=
ng suspension of trading in London in the wake of the short squeeze. More t=
